title: Devanāgarī & the Alphabet Quiz
description: A beginner-friendly quiz on the structure of the Sanskrit alphabet — vowels and consonants, the five mouth positions, the five vargas of stops, semivowels and sibilants, conjuncts, and the alphabetical order that every dictionary follows.

source.author: Charles Wikner
source.note: Grounded in Wikner Lessons 1–3 (alphabet structure, mouth positions) and 7 (conjuncts); the alphabetical-order angle ties to the MW reading guide.

questions.question: What are the two fundamental divisions of the Sanskrit alphabet?
questions.answer: The vowels (svara) and the consonants (vyañjana). svara = 'sound/tone'; vyañjana = an 'adornment' to the sound (a stop or restriction).

questions.question: On what principle is the Sanskrit alphabet arranged?
questions.answer: According to the structure of the mouth — the sounds are ordered by where in the mouth they are produced (the mouth positions), from the back of the throat to the lips.

questions.question: Name the five mouth positions, from the back of the mouth to the front, with their basic vowel.
questions.answer: Guttural (kaṇṭhya, a) → palatal (tālavya, i) → cerebral/retroflex (mūrdhanya, ṛ) → dental (dantya, ḷ) → labial (oṣṭhya, u).

questions.question: At which mouth position is the vowel 'a' produced?
questions.explanation: 'a' is the central, guttural vowel — the whole alphabet is an embellishment of this sound.

questions.question: Vowels can be hrasva, dīrgha, or pluta. What do these mean?
questions.answer: hrasva = short (held one measure, mātrā); dīrgha = long (two measures); pluta = prolonged (three or more measures, mainly Vedic).

questions.question: The compound vowel 'e' is formed using which two mouth positions?
questions.explanation: 'e' arises when 'a' (guttural) is sounded through the 'i' (palatal) position — kaṇṭha-tālavya. 'o' is guttural + labial (kaṇṭhoṣṭhya).

questions.question: The first 25 consonants are 'stops' (sparśa). How are they arranged?
questions.answer: In five sets (varga) of five, by mouth position, each named after its first letter: ka-varga (guttural), ca-varga (palatal), ṭa-varga (cerebral), ta-varga (dental), pa-varga (labial).

questions.question: The pa-varga consonants (pa pha ba bha ma) are stops made at the …?
questions.explanation: Each varga is a set of stops at one position; the pa-varga (labials) close at the lips.

questions.question: After the 25 stops come the four semivowels (antaḥstha). Name them.
Name the three sibilants of Sanskrit (and note why they trip up beginners).
questions.answer: śa (palatal), ṣa (cerebral), sa (dental). They are three distinct letters — a common lookup error is treating them as one 's'.

questions.question: Which single consonant comes last, after the sibilants?
questions.explanation: ha is the final (aspirate) consonant of the alphabet.

questions.question: In Sanskrit alphabetical order, do vowel-initial or consonant-initial words come first?
questions.explanation: The order is: all the vowels (a ā i ī u ū ṛ ṝ ḷ e ai o au), then anusvāra/visarga, then the consonants by varga, semivowels, sibilants, ha.

questions.question: Where do the anusvāra (ṃ) and visarga (ḥ) fall in alphabetical order?
questions.answer: After the vowels and before the consonants — they are listed with the 'sixteen sakti' (the vowels plus aṃ/aḥ), so a word with ṃ/ḥ sorts ahead of consonant-initial material at that point.

questions.question: What is a conjunct consonant (saṃyoga)?
questions.answer: Two or more consonants written together with no intervening vowel — the sign for the first loses its inherent 'a' and the letters are joined into one ligature (e.g. kṣa, jña).

questions.question: The special conjunct क्ष (kṣa) is composed of which two consonants?
questions.explanation: kṣa = ka + ṣa; the other famous special conjunct, jña (ज्ञ), is ja + ña.

questions.question: Why does the mouth-position ordering of the alphabet matter when you use a dictionary?
questions.answer: Because the dictionary's alphabetical order IS this order. a and ā, and ś/ṣ/s, are different letters in different positions — so you must respect the Sanskrit order (and the diacritics) to find a word; this is the single most common first-time lookup mistake.
